skill.name: google-agents-cli-eval
skill.description: This skill should be used when the user wants to run an evaluation, evaluate an ADK agent, write an eval dataset, analyze eval failures, compare eval results, or optimize an agent. It covers the Agent Platform eval methodology and the Quality Flywheel, including eval metrics, dataset schema, LLM-as-judge scoring, and common failure causes.
skill.summary: Guidance for running and analyzing evaluations of Google ADK agents using the Quality Flywheel methodology.
skill.author: google

security_audit.summary: The skill is a pure documentation reference for Google ADK agent evaluation methodology. The 597 static findings are almost entirely false positives triggered by markdown backtick characters used in command-line examples and references to cryptographic hash algorithms in documentation prose. No executable code, scripts, or malicious patterns were found. The critical heuristic combination is a false alarm because this skill has no actual code execution capability—it is only markdown documentation.

low_findings.title: Documentation references to command-line and environment patterns
low_findings.description: The skill markdown files contain backtick-delimited command-line examples and references to environment variables and URLs. These are documentation prose, not actual executable code. The static analyzer flagged them as potential code execution or credential access patterns, but they are educational examples for users running eval commands manually.

content.user_title: Evaluate Google ADK Agents with Confidence
content.value_statement: Running evaluations on AI agents is complex and error-prone without proper guidance. This skill provides structured methodology for creating eval datasets, choosing metrics, analyzing failures, and optimizing agent performance.
actual_capabilities: Guide creation of evaluation datasets following the documented schema
Explain metric selection including LLM-as-judge scoring approaches
Analyze common eval failure causes and suggest remediations
Compare eval results across runs to track agent improvements
Walk through the Quality Flywheel methodology for iterative agent optimization
limitations: Does not execute evaluations itself—it provides guidance only
Does not generate eval datasets automatically; users must write them
Focused on Google ADK agents; not applicable to other agent frameworks

use_cases.title: Run a first evaluation
use_cases.description: New to ADK evals? Get step-by-step guidance to create a dataset, pick metrics, and interpret results.

use_cases.title: Debug failing evals
use_cases.description: Have eval results with failures? Use the skill to identify root causes and apply fixes.

use_cases.title: Optimize an agent iteratively
use_cases.description: Apply the Quality Flywheel to systematically improve agent performance across eval cycles.

prompt_templates.title: Get started with eval basics
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to evaluate my ADK agent for the first time. Walk me through creating a basic eval dataset and choosing the right metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Write an eval dataset
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write an eval dataset for a customer support agent. Include diverse query types and expected response criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze failure patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: My eval run shows 30% failure rate on multi-turn conversations. Analyze likely causes and suggest investigation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Apply the Quality Flywheel
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a systematic iteration loop using the Quality Flywheel: run eval, analyze failures, fix agent, re-evaluate.

output_examples.input: What metrics should I use for a code-generation agent?
output_examples.output: For code-generation agents, recommended metrics include: exact match for simple outputs, pass-rate against unit tests for functional correctness, and LLM-as-judge for code quality and style. Start with pass-rate as your primary metric, then layer in quality scores.

output_examples.input: My agent fails on long conversations. What should I check?
output_examples.output: Common causes for long-conversation failures: context window overflow, instruction drift over turns, and state management bugs. Check: 1) Are earlier messages being truncated? 2) Does the agent maintain consistent persona across turns? 3) Are intermediate tool results properly stored?

best_practices: Start with a small, diverse eval dataset before scaling up
Use LLM-as-judge for subjective quality metrics, exact match for deterministic outputs
Track eval results across iterations to measure improvement quantitatively
anti_patterns: Do not use exact match for open-ended or creative responses
Do not skip failure analysis—every failure reveals an improvement opportunity
Do not evaluate on your training distribution; include edge cases and adversarial inputs

faq.question: What is the Quality Flywheel?
faq.answer: The Quality Flywheel is an iterative methodology: run evals, analyze failures, fix the agent, and re-evaluate. Each cycle improves agent quality through structured feedback.

faq.question: Do I need code to use this skill?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides guidance and methodology. You write and run your own eval code using your preferred tools.

faq.question: What is LLM-as-judge scoring?
faq.answer: LLM-as-judge uses a language model to evaluate another model's outputs against defined criteria. It works well for subjective quality where exact matching is insufficient.

faq.question: How large should my eval dataset be?
faq.answer: Start with 20-50 diverse examples that cover happy paths, edge cases, and failure modes. Expand as you identify gaps.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for non-ADK agents?
faq.answer: The methodology is broadly applicable, but the specific schema and tooling guidance targets Google ADK agents.

faq.question: How do I compare eval runs?
faq.answer: Run the same dataset against different agent versions and compare metric scores. Look for regressions in previously passing cases and improvements in previously failing ones.
